scope.summary: Distinguishes offline credential-store mutations from the authenticated running-service revocation path and advertises both command shapes honestly.
out_of_scope: Implementing the existing control-listener revoke route or its session teardown behavior.
Provisioning a live HTTPS control listener, mTLS certificates, or a live Oracle database.

entry_points.name: oraclemcp --json clients issue
entry_points.trace: client_credential_commands_distinguish_offline_and_live_revocation proves issue output includes the client command, explicit offline-mutation notice, and a POST /operator/v1/client-credentials/revoke mTLS request template with the issued client ID.

entry_points.name: oraclemcp --json clients revoke <client-id>
entry_points.trace: The same focused test proves a lock-open failure selects the online revocation template, preserving a no-downtime path instead of suggesting an unsafe offline write.

entry_points.name: C6 state-store collision fixture
entry_points.trace: c6_cli_vs_running_server starts a separate process owning the real file-store flock and confirms mutating CLI verbs receive the actionable collision result.

limitations: Scoped offline verification passed: cargo fmt -p oraclemcp -- --check; cargo test -p oraclemcp --bin oraclemcp client_credential_commands_distinguish_offline_and_live_revocation -- --nocapture; cargo test -p oraclemcp --test c6_cli_vs_running_server -- --nocapture (2 passed).

No live HTTPS control listener, mTLS identity, live session teardown, or live Oracle environment is claimed; the focused test proves the exact emitted online request contract and C6 proves the real cross-process file-store lock behavior.
